coral-bay-microgrid.summary: Coral Bay is a real isolated community power system operated by Horizon Power. It opened in 2007 as a wind-diesel microgrid with three tilt-up wind turbines and a short-duration ABB PowerStore flywheel; in 2021 Pacific Energy's Hybrid Systems Australia added a 576 kW/343 kWh lithium-ion BESS and controls capable of shutting all diesel units down when wind is sufficient. Horizon Power now describes three 275 kW turbines, multiple low-load diesel generators, the BESS and advanced controls, with wind supplying about 45% of annual electricity.
coral-bay-microgrid.status: Operational, but entering a major renewal cycle. Horizon Power says the ageing wind turbines are approaching end of life and is developing a Future Energy System replacement. Its published program places market engagement in 2025-26, procurement in 2026-27, delivery in 2027-29 and completion around late 2029. No replacement technology, capacity, contractor or investment decision had been announced by the research cutoff, so the future system must not be counted as installed.

organizations.name: Horizon Power
organizations.role: Owner/operator of the isolated electricity system and developer of the current replacement program.

organizations.name: Verve Energy
organizations.role: Historical delivery partner for the 2007 wind-diesel project.

organizations.name: Pacific Energy / Hybrid Systems Australia
organizations.role: 2021 lithium-ion BESS, microgrid-control and integration provider.

organizations.name: ABB
organizations.role: Supplier/integrator of the original PowerStore flywheel and microgrid control architecture.

organizations.name: Vergnet
organizations.role: Supplier of the original tilt-up wind turbines; current Horizon material does not publish the model.

specifications.label: Wind generation
specifications.value: Current owner rating: three 275 kW turbines, 825 kW aggregate. Older documents disagree—ABB says three 200 kW units and the recovered project record says three 225 kW units—so 600/675 kW figures should be treated as historical reporting bases rather than current nameplate.

specifications.label: 2021 battery
specifications.value: 576 kW/343 kWh lithium-ion BESS, approximately 36 minutes at full rated power before losses/reserves. Battery-cell and PCS models are not disclosed.

specifications.label: Historical diesel plant
specifications.value: The recovered technical record identifies seven Detroit Series 60 low-load diesel sets at 320 kW each (2.24 MW aggregate). Horizon's current page confirms multiple low-load diesels but not count, maker or present aggregate rating.

specifications.label: Historical flywheel
specifications.value: ABB PowerStore rated 500 kW and 18 MW-seconds (18 MJ, equivalent to only 5 kWh). The legacy database's '500 kWh flywheel' is a unit error: 500 kW is power, not stored energy.

specifications.label: Load
specifications.value: Historical peak demand about 600 kW and minimum about 200 kW; no current peak or annual demand is public.

specifications.label: Energy mix
specifications.value: Horizon reports wind provides approximately 45% of annual electricity. ABB's older case study reported instantaneous wind shares up to 95%, more than 80% for roughly one-third of the year and above 90% for about 900 hours/year; these are not annual energy shares or current BESS-era measurements.

equipment.detail: Three tilt-up machines; Horizon currently rates each at 275 kW, while older sources use lower ratings and do not provide a reliable model designation.
576 kW/343 kWh lithium-ion system commissioned in 2021 with plant controls; cell and converter OEMs were not published.

500 kW/18 MW-seconds high-power flywheel used in the original configuration. The 2021 battery superseded its grid-forming/high-renewables role, but reviewed sources do not explicitly document disposal date.
Seven 320 kW low-load sets in the recovered historical record; present fleet configuration has not been republished by Horizon.

technicalDetails.detail: The 2021 BESS and controls can turn all diesel generators off during sufficient renewable production and automatically restart/reconnect them as wind falls.
ABB's flywheel and distributed control system smoothed wind variability and supplied fast frequency/voltage support while low-load diesels supplied longer-duration balancing.

The three wind turbines are ageing and Horizon is planning their replacement. Published material defines a staged procurement program but does not yet establish the replacement resource mix or whether existing BESS/diesels will be retained.
Capacity discrepancies and the flywheel unit error come from historical project literature. Current-owner ratings are preferred, and no unsupported conversion of MW-seconds to kWh should be retained.

sources.title: Coral Bay Future Energy System Project
WA first for Coral Bay
Coral Bay battery energy storage system

Recovered Coral Bay Microgrid project record

de-grussa-copper-mine-microgrid.summary: DeGrussa was an off-grid mine hybrid combining 10.6 MWac of single-axis-tracking PV, a 6 MW/1.5 MWh battery and an existing 19 MW diesel station for an 11–13 MW continuous mine load. Commissioned in June 2016, it produced roughly 20.4 GWh/year and met close to 20% of annual mine electricity, with expected savings around five million litres of diesel and 12,000 tonnes CO2 each year. It was a mine-life asset, not a permanent community power system.
de-grussa-copper-mine-microgrid.status: Decommissioned. Sandfire extracted the last stoping ore on 5 October 2022 and moved DeGrussa into care, maintenance and rehabilitation. Neoen then began dismantling the solar-battery plant, and Westforce reports the removal and site rehabilitation completed in 2024. The former project's capacity must no longer be shown as operational; resale/reuse destinations for individual panels, inverters, transformers and battery equipment were not publicly confirmed.

organizations.name: Sandfire Resources
organizations.role: Mine owner and solar-power purchaser/offtaker; now responsible for mine closure and rehabilitation.

organizations.name: DeGrussa Solar Project Pty Ltd / Neoen
organizations.role: Solar-battery project company and owner during operation; initiated dismantling after mine closure.

organizations.name: juwi Renewable Energy
organizations.role: Hybrid developer, EPC/integration contractor and operating-knowledge author.

organizations.name: Kalgoorlie Power Systems
organizations.role: Owner/operator of the pre-existing 19 MW diesel power station.

organizations.name: Australian Renewable Energy Agency
organizations.role: Provided A$20.9 million support and required project knowledge sharing.

organizations.name: OTOC Limited
organizations.role: Construction partner for the original solar project.

organizations.name: Westforce Construction
organizations.role: Contractor for 2024 PV/BESS dismantling, recycling preparation and 240,000 m² site rehabilitation.

organizations.name: ABB / ComAp
organizations.role: Battery-control and InteliSys communications-bridge suppliers named in the commissioning record.

specifications.label: Solar PV
specifications.value: 10.6 MWac on single-axis trackers using 34,080 modules; expected/reported output approximately 20.4 GWh/year.

specifications.label: Battery
specifications.value: Commissioning/project records describe 6 MW/1.5 MWh lithium-ion storage (15 minutes at full power). Westforce's decommissioning page calls the removed BESS 4 MW, likely a different battery-versus-converter reporting basis; the discrepancy is unresolved.

specifications.label: Diesel generation
specifications.value: 19 MW existing isolated-mine station operated by Kalgoorlie Power Systems.

specifications.label: Mine load
specifications.value: Approximately 11–13 MW continuous during the operating-mine period.

specifications.label: Battery interconnection
specifications.value: One 5 MVA battery transformer documented in the integration report.

specifications.label: Cost and support
specifications.value: Approximately A$40 million total project cost, including A$20.9 million ARENA support. These are original-development values, not dismantling costs.

specifications.label: Energy mix and savings
specifications.value: Solar supplied nearly 20% of annual mine electricity; expected annual displacement was about five million litres of diesel and 12,000 tonnes CO2. These operating-period figures ended with mine/project closure.

specifications.label: Decommissioning quantities
specifications.value: Westforce reports removing 34,080 panels, about 850 tonnes of steel and 30 tonnes of copper, then rehabilitating approximately 240,000 m² in 2024.

equipment.detail: 34,080 modules on single-axis trackers; module and inverter models were not identified in the reviewed authoritative sources.
6 MW/1.5 MWh project rating; ABB is explicitly named for the battery-control interface, but the cell manufacturer and exact PCS product are not established by the knowledge report.
Connected the plant-control layers; obsolete bridge firmware caused an intermittent commissioning communications fault.
Coordinated diesel, PV, battery reserve, voltage/reactive-power and ramp-rate limits.

technicalDetails.detail: The control philosophy maintained reserve equal to 1 MW plus 85% of instantaneous PV output across battery and diesel resources.
Ramp-rate control, frequency response, reactive-power/voltage support and spinning reserve allowed high PV penetration on the isolated mine network.
A field communications failure was traced to out-of-date ComAp InteliSys Bridge firmware, an unusually specific public integration lesson.

The plant fulfilled its intended mine-life role for roughly seven years. Dismantling after ore depletion is not evidence of technical failure, but the lack of a post-mine load made continued operation at the site uneconomic.

denver-international-airport.summary: The legacy title is misleading: this is the Peña Station NEXT microgrid at 61st and Peña, an energy district next to Denver International Airport, not an airport-terminal-wide island. The core installation combines a 1.6 MWdc/1.3 MWac airport parking-canopy array, a 259 kWdc/200 kWac Panasonic-rooftop array and Xcel Energy's 1 MW/2 MWh lithium-ion battery. The islanding demonstration initially protects Panasonic's building while also testing feeder-level solar integration and grid services.
denver-international-airport.status: Operational as a district/test-bed asset: Peña Station NEXT's current site says the Xcel-owned battery and solar form an on-site microgrid. The original utility demonstration had a two-year pilot period, but no current public report gives battery state of health, islanding-event duration, annual solar production or whether every original research use case remains enabled. Denver airport's separate 2025 2.2 MW/4.4 MWh AGTS backup battery is a different project and is excluded.

organizations.name: Public Service Company of Colorado / Xcel Energy
organizations.role: Battery owner/operator, serving utility and microgrid demonstration lead.

organizations.name: Panasonic Enterprise Solutions
organizations.role: Anchor tenant, rooftop-PV host and initially islanded building/load.

organizations.name: Denver International Airport / City and County of Denver
organizations.role: Parking-lot/solar-canopy host, land/infrastructure partner and airport capital contributor.

organizations.name: L.C. Fulenwider
organizations.role: Peña Station NEXT master developer and public-private consortium participant.

organizations.name: Younicos
organizations.role: Supplier/integrator of the 1 MW/2 MWh battery and control platform.

organizations.name: Namasté Solar / SunPower
organizations.role: PV construction and equipment partners for the parking canopy and rooftop systems.

organizations.name: National Renewable Energy Laboratory
organizations.role: Cooperative research partner for the wider 400-acre energy-district master plan and modeling.

specifications.label: Parking-canopy PV
specifications.value: 1.6 MWdc/approximately 1.3 MWac over 609 parking spaces at the airport-owned 61st and Peña lot.

specifications.label: Panasonic rooftop PV
specifications.value: 259 kWdc/200 kWac. Combined with the carport, the core solar portfolio is about 1.86 MWdc/1.5 MWac; the legacy 1.3 MW figure captures only the canopy's AC rating.

specifications.label: Battery
specifications.value: 1 MW/2 MWh lithium-ion, two hours at rated output; cell chemistry subtype and cell manufacturer were not disclosed in reviewed public material.

specifications.label: Demonstration scale
specifications.value: CEC's case study totals the PV and battery power resources at approximately 2.86 MW on a nameplate-sum basis. This is not average generation or airport load served.

specifications.label: Cost
specifications.value: Approximately US$10.3 million for the district demonstration excluding Panasonic's rooftop system; Denver separately reported US$4.5 million airport capital for the parking facility, which included 800 spaces and related infrastructure.

specifications.label: Resilience reserve
specifications.value: The study reserved about 20% of battery capacity for approximately four hours of Panasonic-building backup; the remainder supported grid/market use cases.

specifications.label: Energy mix
specifications.value: Solar, stored solar/grid energy and normal Xcel feeder supply. No dedicated fossil generator is identified in the core Peña microgrid, and no current annual renewable fraction is public.

equipment.detail: 1 MW/2 MWh lithium-ion BESS and dispatch/control system owned by Xcel; battery-cell OEM and current usable capacity are not public.
Approximately 1.6 MWdc over 609 of the lot's 800 parking spaces; Namasté Solar participated in construction.
259 kWdc on Panasonic's building; module/inverter model detail is not disclosed in the reviewed sources.
Eleven Level 1 charging stations were included at the airport parking lot. They are loads/amenities, not generation capacity.

technicalDetails.detail: PV ramp smoothing and voltage management, feeder/system peak reduction, energy arbitrage, frequency/regulation service and islanding of the Panasonic building.
The test feeder was described at roughly 30% solar penetration, making distribution-voltage and variability management central research objectives.
The storage/control core was commissioned in roughly 20 weeks under the original pilot, a notable public-private deployment result.

The surrounding Peña Station NEXT district is approximately 400 acres, but the cited evidence does not show that every future building can island. Likewise, airport ownership of the parking canopy does not make this a whole-airport microgrid.
DEN later announced a separate 2.2 MW/4.4 MWh battery to back up the Automated Guideway Transit System. It should not be added to Peña Station's capacity.

deokjeok-island.summary: The legacy record conflates three different Deokjeok Island efforts: the physical KEPCO island grid, a large KT-led 'energy-independent island' proposal announced in 2015, and a tiny KICT research hybrid at Urumsil village. The last detailed whole-island inventory found describes a diesel-dominant 6.9 kV system with 2.9 MW diesel, 169 kW PV, 63 kW wind and no battery. The proposed 500 kW PV/1.5 MW wind/6 MWh ESS project did not progress after a tariff-rule change damaged its economics.
deokjeok-island.status: Mixed identity, with the large project stopped before construction. Reporting in 2018 said none of the five 2015 Korean energy-independent-island projects, including Deokjeok, had formed an SPC or advanced after the 2016 tariff change. A 2021 peer-reviewed study provides the latest authoritative physical inventory of the existing diesel/renewable grid, while the small Urumsil research installation was monitored in 2016-17. No source through the research cutoff verifies that the proposed utility-scale ESS was built or that the experimental village equipment still operates.

organizations.name: Korea Electric Power Corporation (KEPCO)
organizations.role: Operator/utility associated with the island's existing isolated distribution and diesel system.

organizations.name: KT consortium
organizations.role: Selected private consortium for the 2015 energy-independent-island proposal; the project later stalled before an SPC/build.

organizations.name: Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy
organizations.role: National program sponsor that selected the Deokjeok consortium.

organizations.name: Incheon Metropolitan City / Ongjin County
organizations.role: Local-government planning and community partners for the proposed island program and research activities.

organizations.name: Korea Institute of Civil Engineering and Building Technology (KICT)
organizations.role: Developer/researcher of the separate small Urumsil village hybrid renewable system.

specifications.label: Existing whole-island diesel
specifications.value: 2.9 MW from three 300 kW and four 500 kW generators.

specifications.label: Existing whole-island renewables
specifications.value: 169 kW PV plus 63 kW wind (eleven 3 kW and three 10 kW turbines), with no ESS in the surveyed system.

specifications.label: Existing network and load
specifications.value: 6.9 kV, 60 Hz isolated grid with two radial feeders and normally open ties; approximately 1,900 kW maximum, 1,190 kW average and 700 kW minimum load, averaging 28,554 kWh/day in the study data.

specifications.label: Existing energy mix
specifications.value: About 3% annual renewable penetration in the study, with roughly 2.56 million litres/year diesel consumption and diesel providing the overwhelming majority of electricity.

specifications.label: Unbuilt 2015 proposal
specifications.value: Planned 500 kW PV, 1.5 MW wind and 6 MWh ESS over 2016-18 at a projected KRW17.8 billion. These are proposal values, not installed equipment.

specifications.label: Urumsil research hybrid
specifications.value: Components described as two 1.5 kW vertical-axis wind turbines plus 3 kW PV, a 6 kW arithmetic total, with battery storage and pumped-water loads. The same publication inconsistently calls it 24 kW in one passage; no defensible 24 kW equipment breakdown is given.

equipment.detail: Three 300 kW and four 500 kW generator sets; engine/generator OEMs and models are not given in the grid study.
169 kW PV and fourteen small wind turbines totaling 63 kW; exact module, turbine and inverter models are undisclosed.
Two 1.5 kW Darrieus vertical-axis turbines using NACA0015 blades; 3 m blade length, 2 m rotor diameter, 3 m/s cut-in, 13.5 m/s rated wind speed and 300 rpm rated speed.
3 kW PV tilted 30 degrees, battery bank and inverter feeding AC loads; battery chemistry, kWh and equipment vendors were not disclosed.
The planned 1.5 MW wind/500 kW PV/6 MWh ESS package never reached a verified installation; no final OEM selection should be inferred.

technicalDetails.detail: Buk-ri and Seopo-ri radial feeders have normally open interconnection points; modeling evaluated sectionalizing and optimal renewables/storage, but modeled configurations are not as-built assets.

A 2016 change linked island electricity purchase prices to oil prices rather than a fixed tariff, undermining private-project economics. By late 2018 the selected Deokjeok consortium had not established an SPC or proceeded.
Wind acted as the master renewable source; power flowed through battery/inverter stages to village experimental loads including pumping, and the research monitored performance in 2016-17.

The grid paper's optimized high-renewables scenarios are simulations, not construction evidence. The archive's NEDO/geothermal attribution is also unsupported by the project-specific sources reviewed and should not be presented as installed electric generation.

sources.title: Optimal Operation of an Islanded Microgrid with Renewable Generation and Energy Storage
Energy self-reliant island private operators selected
sources.publisher: Government of the Republic of Korea

dharnai-microgrid.summary: Greenpeace commissioned Dharnai's 100 kW solar mini-grid in 2014 as a demonstration for a Bihar village that had lacked reliable grid power. The design split 70 kW among homes, shops and community services and 30 kW among irrigation pumps, with battery-backed distribution and streetlights. It delivered meaningful early social benefits, but it was not durable: grid electricity arrived in 2016, batteries failed after roughly three years, tariffs and load limits were unattractive, and maintenance and collections collapsed.
dharnai-microgrid.status: Defunct as a mini-grid. Field reporting in 2021 found the power station used as a makeshift cattle shed and the PV, CCTV and distribution infrastructure idle; a 2026 sector report classifies the project as failed by 2018. A few stand-alone daytime irrigation pumps were still working in 2021 because they did not depend on the failed battery bank, but that does not make the central mini-grid operational.

organizations.name: Greenpeace India
organizations.role: Project initiator, principal funder, public campaign lead and commissioning reporter.

organizations.name: BASIX
organizations.role: Livelihood/community implementation and operating partner.

organizations.name: Centre for Environment and Energy Development (CEED)
organizations.role: Community mobilization and local implementation partner.

organizations.name: Kripa Solar
organizations.role: Solar installation contractor identified at project foundation.

organizations.name: Village Electricity Committee
organizations.role: Local governance, tariff/collection and community interface described in post-project research.

specifications.label: Solar PV
specifications.value: 100 kW total: 70 kW for household/commercial/community demand and 30 kW allocated to irrigation pumping.

specifications.label: PV module count
specifications.value: Greenpeace's retrospective reports 280 panels; module wattage, manufacturer, inverter model and actual AC capacity were not published.

specifications.label: Initial service scope
specifications.value: Launch reporting cites more than 450 households, about 50 commercial loads, two schools, one health centre, a farmer-training centre and 60 streetlights, serving roughly 2,400 people. Another Greenpeace retrospective says about 400 homes and six pumps, while the launch says ten 3 hp pumps; these are reporting/boundary differences.

specifications.label: Project cost
specifications.value: Approximately INR3 crore at commissioning.

specifications.label: Battery
specifications.value: Battery-backed service was central to night operation, but reliable public sources do not establish chemistry, manufacturer, nominal kWh, usable duration or replacement plan.

specifications.label: Connection decline
specifications.value: A cited village study counted 225 solar connections in 2014 and 126 in 2016, before final collapse.

specifications.label: Tariff competition
specifications.value: Field reporting cites about INR9/kWh for mini-grid power versus INR3/kWh for conventional grid power after grid arrival; service also restricted high-power appliances.

equipment.detail: Approximately 280 rooftop/ground-mounted panels across community facilities; module and inverter OEM/models were not disclosed.
Unspecified battery storage for night/firm service. Exhaustion after roughly three years and lack of repair disabled the central mini-grid; kWh and chemistry remain unverified.
Commissioning material says ten 3 hp solar pumps under a 30 kW allocation; a later Greenpeace account says six. Some battery-free daytime pumps remained in use in 2021.
Local wires, meters, rooftop installations, CCTV/monitoring and 60 solar-powered streetlights were deployed; commercial component models were not published.

technicalDetails.detail: Residents reported improved evening study, cooking and perceived safety, plus fewer snakebite deaths around lit streets. These social benefits were real but concentrated in the first operating years.

Grid power arrived about two years after commissioning; lower tariffs and fewer appliance restrictions drew customers away. Around year three the batteries were exhausted, maintenance was not restored and billing/collection arrangements deteriorated, leaving the system defunct by 2017-18.

The case shows that a technically commissioned donor-backed mini-grid still needs competitive service, replacement reserves, durable O&M, collection authority and rules for coexistence with an arriving central grid.
Daytime solar irrigation can survive independently of battery failure, but isolated functioning pumps must not be used to label the village distribution microgrid operational.

sources.title: Dharnai goes live, powered by Greenpeace's first solar microgrid
Bihar village declares independence from darkness and anonymity
Greenpeace lays foundation for energy independence in Dharnai
Energy for Dharnai
The downfall of solar energy at Dharnai, Bihar's first solar village
Sociotechnical transitions and the Dharnai solar microgrid
Mini-grid Markets, Mechanisms and Monetisation

diavik-mine-wind-diesel-microgrid.summary: Diavik's remote mine power system combined an approximately 46–48 MW Caterpillar diesel station with four 2.3 MW Enercon E-70 cold-climate wind turbines commissioned in 2012. A 3.5 MW, 6,620-panel bifacial solar plant was completed in 2024, evolving the record from wind-diesel to wind-solar-diesel. Wind delivered more than 190 GWh over the operating period and the renewable assets materially reduced annual fuel hauled over the ice road.
diavik-mine-wind-diesel-microgrid.status: Transitioning from production to closure. Rio Tinto delivered the mine's final diamond production on 26 March 2026 and plans active closure work through 2029 followed by monitoring. The solar plant was expressly designed to supply up to 25% of closure-stage electricity, so the hybrid power assets still support closure rather than diamond production. No public decision yet establishes the wind/solar equipment's disposition after closure.

organizations.name: Rio Tinto / Diavik Diamond Mines Inc.
organizations.role: Mine and power-system owner/operator; now leading closure and rehabilitation.

organizations.name: Enercon
organizations.role: Supplier of the four E-70 direct-drive cold-climate wind turbines.

organizations.name: Caterpillar
organizations.role: Supplier of the operating-mine diesel generator fleet.

organizations.name: Solvest Inc.
organizations.role: Solar-project designer/installer.

organizations.name: Tłı̨chǫ Investment Corporation
organizations.role: Indigenous construction partner for the 2024 solar plant.

organizations.name: Natural Resources Canada
organizations.role: Federal technical reporting and clean-energy support source.

specifications.label: Wind farm
specifications.value: Four Enercon E-70 turbines at 2.3 MW each, 9.2 MW aggregate; first power 28 September 2012.

specifications.label: Solar plant
specifications.value: 3.5 MW from 6,620 bifacial panels, completed in 2024; expected generation approximately 4.2 GWh/year.

specifications.label: Diesel station
specifications.value: Detailed trade reporting identifies eleven Caterpillar generators totaling 46.2 MW—nine 4.4 MW plus two 3.3 MW units—while the recovered project record rounds the fleet to 48 MW.

specifications.label: Wind performance
specifications.value: Peak instantaneous wind penetration reached 52%. First-year average penetration was about 7%, displacing 3.8 million litres of diesel; design expectations were roughly 17 GWh/year, 10% annual energy and 12,000 tonnes CO2/year.

specifications.label: Cumulative performance
specifications.value: A federal mining report cites about 191 GWh wind generation and 43.4 million litres of diesel displaced over the reporting period; Rio's current site says total renewable generation exceeded 200 GWh over mine operations.

specifications.label: Solar savings
specifications.value: Expected approximately one million litres diesel and 2,900 tonnes CO2e avoided annually, and up to 25% of electricity demand during closure. These are forecasts, not a published 2025-26 meter result.

specifications.label: Storage
specifications.value: No electrical battery storage is documented in the wind-solar-diesel system; diesel remains the dispatchable firming source.

equipment.detail: Four 2.3 MW gearless/direct-drive cold-climate units with blade heating/de-icing adaptations for temperatures near -40°C.
Nine 4.4 MW and two 3.3 MW units in operating-mine reporting; precise engine/generator models are not provided.
6,620 bifacial modules totaling 3.5 MW. Rio Tinto does not identify module, inverter or tracker OEM/model in its completion release.

technicalDetails.detail: Direct drive reduces gearbox exposure; blade de-icing/heating, low-temperature lubrication and electronics modifications addressed frost and extreme-cold issues encountered in early operation.
Every litre of displaced diesel reduces fuel hauled to the remote mine over a limited winter-road season, giving renewables an operational-resilience value beyond commodity fuel price.

The solar plant's stated 25% share applies to lower closure-stage electricity demand, not historical full-production load. It cannot be added to the historical wind percentage as a current mine-production energy mix.
Diamond extraction is complete, but closure work still needs power through 2029. Asset retirement, resale or community reuse after closure remains unannounced.

sources.title: Rio Tinto's Diavik Diamond Mine delivers its final production
Diavik
Rio Tinto completes construction of solar power plant at Diavik
Diavik Diamond Mine, Northwest Territories
Renewable energy and electrification at Canadian mines
sources.publisher: Government of Canada / Natural Resources Canada

sources.title: Into the wind — Diavik wind farm
sources.publisher: Canadian Institute of Mining, Metallurgy and Petroleum Magazine

sources.title: Recovered Diavik Mine Wind-Diesel Microgrid project record

dnv-gl-internet-things-microgrid.summary: This record was a US$2.15 million ARPA-E NODES software-and-controls R&D project, not a newly built DNV-owned microgrid. DNV GL, Geli and Group NIRE proposed an 'Internet of Energy' platform that could register, aggregate, schedule and dispatch as many as 100 small distributed resources while coordinating market regulation and local distribution support. It was demonstrated at Group NIRE's pre-existing utility-connected test facility at Reese Technology Center in Lubbock.
dnv-gl-internet-things-microgrid.status: Research program completed/alumni. DOE documentation gives an August 2016–August 2019 project term, and ARPA-E now lists NODES and the DNV project as Alumni. The Reese testbed continues in evolved form as Texas Tech/Group NIRE's GLEAMM facility, but no public final report found verifies that the DNV deployment reached 100 devices, quantifies service performance or confirms the original 1 MW/1 MWh battery remains installed. Do not label the R&D award as a currently operating commercial microgrid.

organizations.name: DNV GL (now DNV)
organizations.role: ARPA-E award lead; provided power-system simulation, platform development and independent validation.

organizations.name: Geli
organizations.role: Networking, DER control and market-balancing software partner.

organizations.name: Group NIRE
organizations.role: Host/partner operating the pre-existing Reese utility-connected test facility.

organizations.name: ARPA-E / U.S. Department of Energy
organizations.role: NODES program sponsor and US$2.15 million award funder.

organizations.name: Texas Tech University
organizations.role: Research partner in the Reese testbed and current GLEAMM successor facility.

organizations.name: South Plains Electric Cooperative
organizations.role: Serving utility/interconnection partner for the test facility and original battery project.

specifications.label: ARPA-E award
specifications.value: US$2.15 million under NODES; documented project term 15 August 2016 to 14 August 2019.

specifications.label: DER target
specifications.value: Platform concept for up to 100 distributed resources/controllable loads. This was a scalability target, not verified installed capacity or achieved device count.

specifications.label: Pre-existing battery
specifications.value: 1 MW/1 MWh lithium-ion system commissioned 18 October 2013, about three years before the DNV award term. It was host-testbed infrastructure, not NODES-funded new storage.

specifications.label: Target resource mix
specifications.value: Batteries, EVs, HVAC, water heaters, LED lighting and renewable generation were eligible controllable resources; public sources do not identify the final number or aggregate capacity actually enrolled.

specifications.label: Current GLEAMM inventory
specifications.value: Texas Tech currently lists 150 kW monocrystalline PV, 900 kW wind interconnection, 500 kW diesel, 1 MW electronic load, 180 kW reactive load, 48 kW lead-acid storage, a 30 kW EV simulator and 20 kW data center. This evolved inventory is not the original DNV project's commissioned scope.

equipment.detail: 1 MW, 60-minute system using Samsung SDI lithium-ion batteries, commissioned in 2013; current disposition is not confirmed.
DNV simulation/validation combined with Geli networking, dispatch and market-balancing software; commercial release/version was not disclosed.
Platform integration with the local utility's SCADA/monitoring and control environment; specific gateway, relay and communications products were not published.

GLEAMM's live inventory includes PV, wind interconnection, diesel, programmable loads, small lead-acid storage and EV/data-center simulators; these show continued testbed use, not continuity of every original asset.

technicalDetails.detail: Automated resource registration, scheduling, aggregation, dispatch and performance validation were intended to let behind-the-meter resources support both frequency regulation and distribution reliability.
The research objective was to deliver grid services with minimal interference to each resource's local behind-the-meter demand-management purpose.
ARPA-E's live portfolio classifies NODES and the DNV project as Alumni; no public operational KPI, commercial rollout or verified 100-device demonstration result was located.
The 2013 battery and present GLEAMM equipment belong to the host test facility. They must not be reported as hardware purchased by the 2016 DNV NODES award.

east-hampton-microgrid-feasibility-study.summary: The Long Island Community Microgrid Project was a 2015–16 NY Prize Stage 1 feasibility study, not a constructed microgrid. Its preferred concept would have coordinated up to 15 MW of solar, a central 5 MW/25 MWh battery, three smaller facility batteries, existing 6 MW substation peakers and critical-site generators across two East Hampton feeders. The resilient boundary included water pumping and fire protection plus thousands of customers, far beyond the police-substation address in the legacy record.
east-hampton-microgrid-feasibility-study.status: Feasibility completed; proposed system not built. East Hampton was absent from the eleven NY Prize Stage 2 awardees, and the Town's current renewable-energy roadmap still describes LICMP as a 2016 study while recommending that the Town develop future large-scale microgrid partnerships. A separate NextEra 5 MW/40 MWh utility battery entered service in East Hampton in 2018, but it is not the proposed 5 MW/25 MWh LICMP community microgrid and must remain a separate record.

organizations.name: Town of East Hampton
organizations.role: NY Prize applicant, community sponsor and current energy-roadmap owner.

organizations.name: Clean Coalition
organizations.role: Lead technical/project-development consultant for the LICMP feasibility study.

organizations.name: PSEG Long Island / Long Island Power Authority
organizations.role: Serving utility, distribution/SCADA partner and owner/operator context for proposed substation resources.

organizations.name: New York State Energy Research and Development Authority
organizations.role: NY Prize Stage 1 study funder and administrator.

organizations.name: Suffolk County Water Authority
organizations.role: Critical-facility partner for Bridgehampton and Oak View pumping stations.

organizations.name: Springs Fire District
organizations.role: Critical-facility/first-responder partner in the proposed resilient boundary.

organizations.name: NextEra Energy Resources
organizations.role: Developer of the separate 5 MW/40 MWh East Hampton Energy Storage Center commissioned in 2018.

specifications.label: Proposed solar
specifications.value: Up to 15 MW: 10 MW of separately planned third-party PV plus 5 MW additional PV enabled/optimized by the microgrid. None is verified as built under LICMP.

specifications.label: Proposed central storage
specifications.value: 5 MW/25 MWh at the East Hampton gas-turbine substation, five-hour nameplate duration, plus three smaller local facility batteries whose final ratings were not selected for construction.

specifications.label: Existing backup generation in study
specifications.value: Three 2 MW distillate-fueled substation generators (6 MW aggregate) plus site emergency generators with roughly two days' fuel. These were existing/secondary backup, not new renewable capacity.

specifications.label: Proposed customer/critical-load boundary
specifications.value: Two feeders serving two water-pumping stations, Springs Fire District, about 100 commercial/industrial customers and approximately 3,200 residential customers; study context cites water service for roughly 40,000 people.

specifications.label: Modeled solar energy
specifications.value: Approximately 17.8 MWh/day from 5 MW new PV, 35.6 MWh/day from the 10 MW portfolio and 53.4 MWh/day at 15 MW, compared with about 27.3 MWh/day of secondary-priority demand.

specifications.label: Independent BCA cost
specifications.value: Approximately US$40.4 million lifecycle cost: US$34.9 million capital, US$60,000 planning and US$5.47 million present-value fixed O&M; the central 5 MW/25 MWh battery accounted for roughly US$20 million installed/service cost over 20 years.

specifications.label: Benefit-cost result
specifications.value: Without a specific avoided transmission upgrade, BCR 0.4 and NPV -US$24.7 million; with that deferral credited, BCR 1.1, NPV +US$4.04 million and IRR 8.2%. The no-deferral case needed about 6.6 outage days/year to break even.

specifications.label: Separate operating BESS
specifications.value: East Hampton Energy Storage Center: 5 MW/40 MWh lithium-ion, commercial operation August 2018. It is a utility peak/resilience asset from a separate South Fork procurement, not LICMP's 25 MWh conceptual battery.

equipment.detail: 5 MW/25 MWh at the East Hampton substation, with battery/inverter OEM and chemistry to be selected in a later stage that did not occur.
Local PV, storage and controllers at three critical sites, capable of local autonomous operation if communications failed; final vendors and ratings were not procured.
Three 2 MW distillate generator units at the substation, retained as secondary/emergency supply in the study; exact engine models are not disclosed.
5 MW/40 MWh lithium-ion East Hampton Energy Storage Center commissioned in 2018; listed here only to prevent conflation, not as LICMP equipment.

technicalDetails.detail: A central substation controller would coordinate utility-owned/front-of-meter DER through PSEG Long Island SCADA and hardwired/leased telecommunications, with facility controllers able to island locally on communications loss.
The central battery was modeled near 50% state of charge before an event, and conservative P10 solar production was used for resilience planning rather than average-weather output.
Critical installations were to be designed around 130 mph wind and one-in-500-year flood criteria.
The project did not appear among the eleven NY Prize Stage 2 winners. The current Town roadmap's future-tense recommendation to develop large microgrids corroborates that LICMP remained a study.

The legacy text mentions wind, but the final quantified study configuration centers on solar, batteries and existing backup generation. South Fork Wind and the 2018 5 MW/40 MWh battery are separate projects.
